_meta.purpose: Mor-category-level prompt instructions plumbed into the four ETF analysis prompts (Past Returns / Cost & Team / Risk / Future Outlook) as `categoryInstructions`. The same rendered block goes into all four prompts when the fund's Mor category has an entry here.
_meta.coverageRule: Populated for every canonical Mor category in etf-analysis-categories.json (US Morningstar-style and Canada Stock-Analysis-style names alike). Lookups canonicalize the raw per-country category through etf-category-aliases.json first, so an aliased Canada label (e.g. "Financials" -> "Financial", "Information Technology" -> "Technology") resolves to the same entry as its US equivalent.
_meta.instructionStyle: Each category carries three lists. `mostImportant` (3-5 bullets) — qualitative facts describing what this KIND of fund is: its index/selection methodology, the character of the resulting portfolio, and its income & tax nature. Neutral context with no verdict and no numeric thresholds; these are the facts the Key Facts prompt asks the LLM to surface for a specific ETF, in the form 'the fact — its character'. `greenFlags` and `redFlags` (3-5 bullets each) are the evaluative lists, in the form 'signal — why it matters' with brief numeric anchors (tickers, bps, %). Rules: (1) include ONLY genuinely impactful signals — omit obvious basics like a plain low/high expense ratio; (2) `redFlags` must NOT be the mirror-image negation of `greenFlags` — each red flag is its own distinct failure mode, so a given dial appears on at most one side.
_meta.keyConvention: Keys MUST be category slugs — slugifyEtfCategory(name) from etf-categorization-utils, e.g. 'Large Blend' -> 'large-blend', 'Trading--Leveraged Equity' -> 'trading-leveraged-equity'. This is the same identifier used in category URLs and getEtfCategoryBySlug. Each slug MUST resolve to a categories[].name in etf-analysis-categories.json; unknown keys are silently ignored.

mostImportant: The kind of index it tracks and how it picks and weights holdings — a rules-based, passively cap-weighted broad index (e.g. S&P 500, CRSP US Total Market) with no discretionary stock-picking.

The character of the resulting portfolio — broadly diversified but dominated by the largest companies, so its behaviour leans on a handful of mega-caps and the sectors they sit in (currently mega-cap tech).
The nature of its income — distributions are mostly qualified dividends rather than ordinary income.
greenFlags: Net cost below the headline expense ratio from rebated securities-lending income — quietly lets the fund track at or just above the index (VOO/VTI/IVV).
Near-zero realised capital-gains distributions over 5Y — strong in-kind redemption discipline that compounds in a taxable account.
Low index-reconstitution turnover — fewer forced taxable trades than rules-based peers.
redFlags: Top-10 weight past ~35% — a 'diversified' fund that's quietly a concentrated mega-cap bet.
Tracking drift above ~25 bps — usually an expired fee waiver or basket drift, and it's pure lost return.
A mid-life benchmark switch or widened sampling — the basket no longer matches the name you bought.

mostImportant: How the index defines and selects "growth" — a rules-based screen on traits like sales/earnings growth, momentum and high valuation, with the exact definition varying by provider (CRSP / Russell / S&P).
The character of the resulting portfolio — growth screens cluster holdings in tech and communication-services names, giving it a pronounced sector personality rather than broad balance.
The nature of its return and income — return is expected almost entirely from price appreciation, with a structurally low dividend yield.
greenFlags: High realised growth-factor loading (forward P/E, EPS-growth) vs the index — you own the growth tilt you pay for (VUG/SCHG), not closet blend.
Matches or beats Russell 1000 Growth net of fees over 5-10Y — the strategy earns its keep after costs.
Reconstitutes more than once a year — keeps the tilt from decaying mid-year as names cross the growth/blend line.
redFlags: Top-10 weight 55-60% in mega-cap tech with no cap — a concentrated sector bet disguised as 'growth'.
Style drift toward blend/quality — you pay growth fees for blend exposure without noticing.
Above ~0.30% with no active mandate — the fee quietly loses to low-cost peers over time.

mostImportant: How the index defines and selects "value" — a rules-based screen on traits like low price-to-book, low P/E and high dividend yield, with definitions differing more between providers than for blend funds.
The character of the resulting portfolio — value screens tilt holdings toward financials, healthcare, energy and industrials, giving it a defensive/cyclical sector personality.
The nature of its income — it carries a structurally higher dividend yield than the broad market, so more of the total return arrives as income.
greenFlags: Lower P/B and higher dividend yield than the S&P 500 in the actual holdings — real value (VTV/IUSV), not just a label.
A quality/profitability screen layered on cheapness — filters out the value traps that drag pure-cheap funds.
Multi-year consecutive dividend growth in dividend-tilt funds — durable payout health, not yield chasing.
redFlags: Sector weights that mirror the broad market — value in name only; won't perform when value rotates.
Lagging Russell 1000 Value by >100 bps annualised over 10Y — a wrong-basket failure, worse than value being out of favour.
A few cheap-but-deteriorating names dominating — value traps, cheap because the business stalled, not because they're mispriced.

mostImportant: The index and size band it tracks — a rules-based cap-weighted mid-cap index (e.g. Russell Midcap, S&P 400) sitting between large- and small-cap, with no stock-picking.
The character of the resulting portfolio — companies large enough to be established yet still growing, more cyclical and far less mega-cap-concentrated than large blend.
The nature of its income and turnover — a modest dividend yield, with periodic turnover as names graduate up to large-cap or fall to small-cap.
greenFlags: Holdings stay inside the Russell Midcap / S&P 400 band — you actually get the mid-cap premium (VO/IJH), not large/small drift.
Low realised cap-gains distributions over 5Y — uncommon mid-cap in-kind discipline, a real hidden tax edge.
Full or near-full replication, not thin sampling — tighter tracking where sampling error runs larger.
redFlags: Drift up into large-cap or down into small-cap — the fund quietly becomes something you didn't buy.
AUM under ~$200M — mid-cap spreads widen fast and tax round-trips invisibly.
Heavy weight in names about to graduate to large-cap — you're buying tomorrow's large-caps, diluting the mid-cap exposure.

mostImportant: How the index selects mid-cap "growth" — a rules-based growth screen applied within the mid-cap size band, with the definition varying by provider.
The character of the resulting portfolio — faster-growing mid-sized companies with a tech / consumer / industrial tilt and higher volatility than mid blend.
The nature of its return — return comes mainly from price appreciation, with a minimal dividend yield.
greenFlags: Strong realised growth loading (P/E, EPS-growth) on top of the mid-cap band — captures the actual mid-growth premium (IJK/VOT).
Stays inside the Russell Midcap / S&P 400 band — resists the pull up into large-cap mega-cap tech.
Low realised cap-gains distributions — mid-cap funds often lack large-cap in-kind discipline, so disciplined tax management is a real edge.
redFlags: A single mid-cap name dominating the top-10 — a closet single-stock bet or an imminent migration out of the band.
Large-cap creep in the top holdings — you're buying tomorrow's large-caps, not mid-caps.
Above ~0.40% with no active mandate — most active mid-growth funds don't beat passive net of fees.

mostImportant: How the index selects mid-cap "value" — a rules-based value screen (low P/B, low P/E, higher yield) applied within the mid-cap band.
The character of the resulting portfolio — cheaper, more cyclical mid-sized companies tilted toward financials, industrials and real estate.
The nature of its income — a higher dividend yield than mid blend, with more of the return arriving as income.
greenFlags: A profitability screen on top of cheapness — the single biggest quality lever; without it you hold low-quality 'zombie' names.
Genuinely lower P/B and higher dividend yield vs the index — the value premise is in the holdings, not the label.
Dividend stability in dividend-tilt sleeves — multi-year payout growth signals the cheap names aren't distressed.
redFlags: Sector mix that mirrors broad mid-cap — value in name only.
Cheap names with stalling fundamentals (value traps) — they bite harder in mid-cap than large-cap.
Drift down into small-cap — drawdowns get materially deeper than mid-cap value.

mostImportant: The index and size band it tracks — a rules-based small-cap index (e.g. Russell 2000, S&P 600), where the choice matters because the S&P 600 applies a profitability filter and the Russell 2000 does not.
The character of the resulting portfolio — many small, less-established companies, more economically sensitive and more volatile than mid or large blend.
The nature of its trading and income — wider spreads and a meaningful annual reconstitution, with a low dividend yield.
greenFlags: Built on a profitability-filtered index (S&P 600) — that screen is why it has historically beaten Russell 2000 by ~2 pp annualised.
Stays inside the Russell 2000 / S&P 600 band (VB/IJR) — captures the small-cap premium with spreads kept tight despite harder microstructure.
Low turnover through the annual Russell rebuild — sidesteps the well-known late-June rebalance drag.
redFlags: Drift down into micro-cap — amplifies drawdowns well beyond what a 'small-cap' label implies.
AUM under ~$200M — small-cap spreads widen and tax round-trips invisibly.
Tracking drift above ~30 bps — small-cap funds miss more than large-cap, but top funds don't miss by that much.

mostImportant: How the index selects small-cap "growth" — a rules-based growth screen applied within the small-cap band.
The character of the resulting portfolio — fast-growing but often early-stage or pre-profit companies, giving high return dispersion and volatility.
The nature of its return — return is almost entirely price appreciation, with little to no dividend.
greenFlags: Strong realised growth loading (EPS, sales growth) vs the index — captures the factor, not just volatile small names.
Stays inside the small-cap band (IJT/VBK) — keeps you where the growth premium actually lives, not drifting to mid-cap.
Caps single-name weight — no one story stock can sink the fund on a single miss.
redFlags: A large pre-profit 'story stock' share — common in Russell 2000 Growth and the first thing to sell off in a drawdown.
Worst calendar-year drawdown 5+ pp deeper than the benchmark — signals micro-cap or concentration drift.
Drift up into mid-cap — the fund stops doing the small-growth job you bought.

mostImportant: How the index selects small-cap "value" — a rules-based value screen within the small-cap band, with some funds adding a profitability filter.
The character of the resulting portfolio — cheap, smaller, cyclical companies tilted to financials, industrials and real estate; historically the home of the size-plus-value premium.
The nature of its income and risk — a higher dividend yield than small blend, with deeper drawdowns in stress.
greenFlags: A profitability filter (AVUV-style) — historically the decisive edge over pure cheap-P/B small value.
Lower P/B and higher dividend yield vs the index while inside the small-cap band — real value where it has paid off.
Cheap names that still generate cash (dividends/buybacks) — a sign they're undervalued, not just optically cheap.
redFlags: Drift down into micro-cap — masks much deeper worst-year drawdowns (the category fell ~35% intraday in 2020).
Sector weights mirroring broad small-cap — value in name only.
Above ~0.40% with no active mandate — hard to justify vs AVUV-style active or cheap passive peers.

mostImportant: The geography and index it tracks — a rules-based cap-weighted index of large developed-market companies outside the US (e.g. MSCI EAFE, FTSE Developed ex-US), with the country/region scope defined by the index.
Whether and how currency is handled — returns include foreign-currency exposure unless the fund explicitly hedges it back to USD.
The nature of its income and tax — typically a higher dividend yield than the US market, subject to foreign withholding tax.
greenFlags: A clear, stable currency-hedge policy (hedged or unhedged, not switching) — hedging drives most of the return gap between otherwise-identical funds.
Transparent disclosure of the foreign-withholding-tax drag — a real cost hidden from the expense ratio.
Country/region weights matching the named index tightly (IXUS/VEA/SCHF) — you own exactly the basket disclosed.
redFlags: A hedge that switches on and off — a backtest, not a strategy.
Geography that doesn't match the label — e.g. includes or excludes Canada/EM differently than you assumed.
Large mid-day premiums/discounts during European/Asian hours — weak market-making while the underlying market is closed.

mostImportant: The index and country scope it tracks — a rules-based cap-weighted emerging-markets index (e.g. MSCI EM, FTSE EM), where the included countries and China-share definition vary by provider.
The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated in a few large countries (China, Taiwan, India), held via local shares and/or ADRs, and carrying currency exposure.
The nature of its risk — sensitive to single-country political, currency and trading-hours factors far more than a developed-market fund.
greenFlags: An explicit single-country cap (China+Taiwan below ~30-35%) — keeps 'diversified' actually diversified (IEMG/VWO/SCHE).
Deep liquidity ($5B+, tight spreads) even during the EM open-hours mismatch — holds up in stress.
Rules-based, verifiable country weights — no discretionary single-country bets you can't see.
redFlags: No single-country cap — cap-weighted EM can run 50-60% in 2-3 names, a concentrated country bet.
Heavy direct local-share holdings — foreign trading-hours and settlement operational risk vs ADRs.
Large NAV mark-downs during stress when underlying markets are closed — common in smaller EM funds.

mostImportant: How the fund defines "technology" — the index's sector boundaries (pure software/semis vs a broad definition that sweeps in internet and some consumer names) decide what you actually own.
The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated and cap-weighted, so a handful of mega-cap tech names usually dominate.
The nature of its behaviour — a single-sector, high-beta fund whose return rides the tech cycle rather than the broad market.
greenFlags: Discloses the software/semis/internet split (XLK/VGT/FTEC) — you know the exact tech-cycle bet you're taking.
Tracks the broad tech cycle (Nasdaq 100 direction) — moves with tech without hidden sub-sector overshoots.
Rebalances on a clear quarterly schedule — new leaders enter promptly instead of leaving you with last cycle's winners.
redFlags: Top-10 weight 60-70% — essentially a mega-cap tech bet, not broad tech.
A 'tech' definition that sweeps in Amazon/Tesla/Meta — overlaps with your Large Growth holdings unknowingly.
Above ~0.50% with no thematic mandate — broad tech doesn't beat low-cost peers at that fee.

mostImportant: What it holds — predominantly equity REITs selected by a rules-based real-estate index, sometimes including specialised or mortgage REITs.
The character of the resulting portfolio — exposure spread across property sub-sectors (residential, industrial, retail, healthcare, data-centre) whose mix drives its behaviour.
The nature of its income and tax — high distributions that are largely non-qualified (taxed as ordinary income), and notable interest-rate sensitivity.
greenFlags: Pure-play equity REITs (>=90%; VNQ/USRT/SCHH) — clean REIT exposure without dilution.
Multi-year consecutive distribution growth — signals tenant and debt health in the portfolio.
Spread across property sub-sectors (residential/industrial/healthcare/data-centre) — no single property cycle can dominate the fund.
redFlags: Mortgage REITs (mREITs) in the basket — materially change duration and rate-sensitivity vs what you expected.
A distribution cut — an early warning of tenant or debt stress, not a one-off.
A rate-shock drawdown deeper than the category (~25-30% in 2022) — concentration or sub-sector issues.

mostImportant: How the fund defines "healthcare" — its sub-sector mix (broad vs pharma-only vs biotech-only) sets the risk and return character.
The character of the resulting portfolio — cap-weighted, so large pharma and managed-care names usually anchor it, with biotech adding binary event risk.
The nature of its behaviour — a defensive sector with steady cash generation, exposed to regulatory and patent-cycle events.
greenFlags: Explicit sub-sector mix (broad vs pharma-pure vs biotech-only; XLV/VHT/IYH) — you know the basket and the risk.
Stays in the top two quartiles across 1Y/3Y/5Y — consistent, not a one-cycle fluke.
Steady cash generation from the payer and big-pharma sleeves — defensive ballast when biotech sells off.
redFlags: Single-name weight above ~5% in a cap-weighted fund — amplifies binary FDA-approval and patent-cliff events.
Top-10 above ~40% in a 'broad' health fund — hidden pharma-mega-cap concentration.
Bouncing between top and bottom quartile across cycles — a hidden sub-sector tilt the label hides.

mostImportant: What it holds — a rules-based investment-grade bond index blending Treasuries, agency MBS and IG corporates (e.g. Bloomberg US Aggregate).
The character of the portfolio — intermediate duration, so its main driver is interest-rate moves rather than default risk.
The nature of its income and tax — steady taxable interest income, with structurally low credit risk.
greenFlags: Credit mix matching the 'core' label (~70-80% Treasury / agency-MBS / IG corporate; AGG/BND/SCHZ) — you own core, not a tilt.
Duration held near the Agg's ~5-7Y — delivers core-bond rate exposure cleanly.
Large-sample replication of the Agg's ~12,000 bonds — keeps tracking tight where sampling error can creep in.
redFlags: Duration drifting to 8+ years — a hidden rate bet you didn't sign up for in 'core'.
Undisclosed HY or EM debt — dilutes the 'core' label and adds credit risk retail isn't pricing.
A loss much deeper than the -13% Agg year (2022) — signals credit or duration drift.

mostImportant: What it holds — short-maturity Treasury and investment-grade corporate bonds selected by a rules-based index.
The character of the portfolio — low duration, so it reprices quickly to rate changes and moves little in price.
The nature of its role — a low-volatility income / cash-parking sleeve paying taxable interest.
greenFlags: Duration held at 1-3Y from Treasury + IG corporate (BSV/SHY/VGSH) — low-duration income paid for by duration risk only.
Low realised trading cost despite continuous bond rolling — turnover is high here and quietly eats return if managed poorly.
Duration short enough to reprice quickly when the Fed moves — the yield adjusts within months, not years.
redFlags: Duration drifting to 4+ years — a hidden rate bet in a 'short-term' fund.
Reaching into HY for yield — the headline yield isn't paid for by short-duration risk.
Yield below a high-yield savings account net of fees — no reason to hold unless you're betting rates fall.

mostImportant: What it holds — very short-maturity Treasury, IG corporate and sometimes structured paper, under a rules-based or lightly-active mandate.
The character of the portfolio — near-cash duration, so price barely moves; it behaves like a cash alternative.
The nature of its income — a modest yield over money-market funds, paid as taxable interest, with minimal NAV variability.
greenFlags: Effective duration under 1Y (BIL/SGOV/USFR) — behaves like cash with a small premium.
Yield clearly beating HYSA net of fees — with the minor 1-3 cent NAV moves disclosed up front (it isn't a $1 money-market fund).
Penny spreads and same- or next-day liquidity — usable as a genuine cash sleeve.
redFlags: Duration above ~1Y — misleading for 'ultrashort'; check the figure, not the label.
CLO-AAA above ~20% — changes the risk character from Treasury-only; not the cash-equivalent the label implies.
Above ~0.20% expense — fees eat directly into the thin premium over cash.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade corporate bonds selected by a rules-based index and weighted by amount issued.
The character of the portfolio — issuance-weighting tilts it toward the largest debt issuers (notably financials), at intermediate-to-long duration.
The nature of its income — a higher taxable yield than Treasuries, paid for with credit and duration risk rather than default-heavy risk.
greenFlags: Stays strictly IG without crossover BB names for extra yield — you're not taking hidden HY risk.
Duration matching the named benchmark — your rate exposure is the one intended.
Broad replication across thousands of issuers — tight tracking without single-issuer surprises.
redFlags: A heavy BBB tilt — loses materially more in credit-stress years than the 'IG' label implies.
Financials concentration 35-45% from cap-by-issuance — more of a financials-credit bet than expected.
A loss beyond the ~13-18% IG drawdown of 2022 — long-duration drift or low-BBB concentration.

mostImportant: What it holds — below-investment-grade ("junk") corporate bonds selected by a rules-based index, often via sampling rather than full replication.
The character of the portfolio — credit-driven, so its return and risk come from default and spread risk far more than from interest rates.
The nature of its income — a high taxable yield that compensates for real default risk, with equity-like drawdowns in credit stress.
greenFlags: Captures fallen angels (downgraded IG) — they historically outperform original-issue HY because the market over-sells the downgrade.
Publishes the CCC % and sector breakdown plainly (HYG/JNK/USHY) — you can see the real risk being taken.
Spread over comparable IG in line with peers — the yield is paid for with measured, disclosed credit risk.
redFlags: Single-sector concentration above ~25% (often energy) — a sector bet hiding inside a 'diversified HY' label.
Heavy sampling (~600 of ~2,500 bonds) with high turnover — slippage and trading cost quietly erode the spread.
A headline yield well above peers — almost always extra CCC exposure, not selection skill.

mostImportant: What it holds — a diversified, national set of investment-grade municipal bonds selected by a rules-based index, at intermediate duration.
The character of the portfolio — high credit quality, with rate sensitivity as the main risk and thinner liquidity than Treasuries in stress.
The nature of its income and tax — interest is federally tax-exempt, so it is best judged on tax-equivalent yield for a given bracket.
greenFlags: Quotes tax-equivalent yield (TEY) at a realistic bracket plus state exemption (MUB/VTEB/TFI) — lets you compare honestly to taxable bonds.
Duration matched to the intermediate benchmark — clean tax-exempt rate exposure.
Broad issuer diversification across thousands of munis — limits single-issuer default impact.
redFlags: Material AMT-bond exposure — should be near zero; it matters specifically for the high-income holders muni targets.
A BBB/non-rated tilt-down — bites in stress windows when muni liquidity thins (spreads 10-50 bps vs 1-5 for Treasuries).
Above ~0.30% expense — hard to justify vs 0.05-0.10% passive muni peers.

mostImportant: What it does — uses swaps and futures to deliver a multiple (e.g. 2x/3x) of an index's return for a single day, reset daily.
The character of the product — daily resetting means multi-day returns compound and diverge from the stated multiple, especially in choppy markets (decay).
The nature of its intended use — a short-term trading tool, not a buy-and-hold investment, carrying high fees and financing cost.
greenFlags: Hits ~3x (or its stated multiple) of the daily move within tight tolerance (TQQQ/SPXL/SOXL) — does the one job it exists for.
$5-25B AUM with billions in daily volume — deep enough to actually trade the thesis.
A transparent, published daily-reset methodology — no hidden path-dependency beyond the structural daily reset.
redFlags: Marketed or held as buy-and-hold — daily-reset compounding makes multi-month return diverge sharply from N x the index.
AUM under ~$500M — unusable for short-term trading; spreads eat the directional edge.
Fees above ~1.20% — pay for nothing extra in this category.

mostImportant: What it does — uses derivatives to deliver the inverse (e.g. -1x/-2x/-3x) of an index's daily return, reset daily.
The character of the product — daily reset causes compounding decay, so it erodes over time in flat or choppy markets even when the directional call is right.
The nature of its intended use — a short-term hedging / trading instrument, not a long-term short position.
greenFlags: Hits the stated inverse multiple within tight tolerance daily (SQQQ/SDS/SPXS) — the entire job of the product.
$1-5B AUM with high daily volume — tradable for tactical hedging.
Low embedded financing/borrow cost on the short side — less hidden daily drag eating the hedge.
redFlags: Used as buy-and-hold 'protection' — compounding decay erodes it in flat/choppy markets even when your call is right.
AUM under ~$200M — effectively un-tradable; spreads and execution costs dominate.
Fees above ~1.20% — hard to justify for a tactical tool.

mostImportant: What it does — holds an equity portfolio and sells options (covered calls or an index-option overlay) to convert potential upside into current income.
The character of the product — it caps upside in exchange for yield, so total return tends to lag a rising market and the income varies with the volatility regime.
The nature of its income and tax — a high headline distribution whose composition (qualified dividends, option income, return-of-capital) drives the real after-tax yield.
greenFlags: Total return (price + distributions) keeps pace with the underlying over a full cycle — it isn't just converting growth into taxable income.
Distributions classified as qualified dividends or capital gains rather than ordinary income — materially better after tax.
Performed sensibly across both low-vol and high-vol regimes — not curve-fit to one environment.
redFlags: Steadily declining price-only NAV beside a high headline yield — the 'income' is partly your own capital coming back.
A high return-of-capital (ROC) share in the 1099 year after year — capital handed back dressed as yield.
Opaque option mechanics — no disclosure of % overwritten, strikes, or roll, so you can't price the upside you give up.

mostImportant: What it does — uses a layered options structure to deliver a defined payoff (a downside buffer and a capped upside) over a set outcome period.
The character of the product — the buffer and cap apply in full only if held from the start to the end of the outcome period; mid-period the payoff differs.
The nature of its use — a structured, outcome-shaping holding tied to an outcome-period calendar, not a continuously-compounding fund.
greenFlags: Laddered series across multiple outcome periods — you're not locked to a single cap window, diluting entry-timing risk.
States plainly that buffer + cap realise only at period end, net of fees — sets the holding-period expectation correctly.
Buffer-vs-floor structure and the cap-reset rule disclosed clearly — you know your protection and your ceiling.
redFlags: Bought or sold mid-period — you get a completely different payoff than the headline buffer + cap.
Active-ratchet / dynamic resets with an opaque trigger — you can't tell when your terms change.
Fees above ~1.00% — above the 0.65-0.85% norm, paying for option-spread and admin overhead.

mostImportant: What it does — holds equities alongside an options hedge (collar, put-spread or buffer) to reduce downside in exchange for giving up some upside.
The character of the product — designed to lag in strong bull markets and cushion drawdowns, with how the hedge is financed deciding how much upside is given up.
The nature of its use — a risk-managed equity sleeve whose payoff depends on the specific hedge structure and roll schedule.
greenFlags: Discloses how the hedge is financed (paid outright, call-sale, or spreads) — you can see the bull-market lag you're paying for.
Laddered or rolling hedges with no gaps — protection is always on, not lapsing between expirations.
Historical drawdowns landed near the stated buffer/floor — the hedge actually worked when it was needed.
redFlags: Sold as 'full upside with downside protection' — overselling; real funds disclose a meaningful bull-market lag.
A put-spread collar whose short put caps protection at a floor — losses below that floor are unhedged.
Fees above ~1.00% — above the 0.50-0.85% norm for the structure.

mostImportant: What lands a fund here — it tracks a single country or narrow regional sleeve (e.g. India, Brazil, Mexico, frontier markets) that doesn't fit a named Morningstar region, so the index is country-specific rather than broadly diversified.
The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated in one economy's banks, state-linked champions and commodity names, so country-specific policy, currency and political risk dominate returns.
The nature of its income and tax — foreign withholding taxes apply at the source-country rate and distributions are unqualified, so the headline yield overstates what reaches a taxable account.
greenFlags: A liquid, exchange-traded local market with a deep underlying basket — full physical replication (e.g. INDA, EWZ) rather than swaps or P-notes, so you own the stocks not a derivative wrapper.
Reasonable single-name caps despite a shallow market — top-10 held near ~40% keeps one state bank or oil major from being the whole fund.
Tracking difference within ~50 bps of the local index after withholding — shows the manager reclaims treaty rates rather than leaking yield.
redFlags: Heavy use of participatory notes or total-return swaps to access the market — adds counterparty risk and a hidden spread on top of the expense ratio.
A persistent premium to NAV above ~1% when the local market is closed during US hours — you're buying stale, marked-up exposure.
Capital controls or repatriation limits in the domicile — redemptions can gate and the price can decouple from fair value for weeks.

mostImportant: What the index covers — large-cap stocks worldwide INCLUDING the US, blending value and growth, so US mega-caps typically make up 55-65% of the basket alongside developed and some emerging names.
The character of the resulting portfolio — it behaves much like a US-heavy world index, so the 'global' label hides that returns are still driven mainly by US tech and the dollar.
The nature of its income — a mix of qualified US dividends and foreign distributions subject to withholding, so part of the yield is recoverable via the foreign tax credit and part is not.
greenFlags: True all-cap-of-the-world breadth with US weight near global market weight (~60%) — VT-style neutrality rather than a closet US-only or closet-EAFE tilt.
Passes through the foreign tax credit on its 1099 — recovers a slice of withholding that an in-fund-blocked structure would forfeit.
Single-digit annual turnover from float-adjusted cap weighting — minimal forced taxable trades despite global rebalancing.
redFlags: US weight drifting well below global market weight without a stated mandate — an unannounced ex-US bet sold as 'global'.
Trading at a premium when both US and overseas markets aren't simultaneously open — intraday price relies on stale foreign marks.
Currency exposure left fully unhedged and undisclosed — a rising dollar can erase the ex-US sleeve's local gains with no warning in the materials.

mostImportant: How it selects — large-cap developed-market stocks OUTSIDE the US screened on value traits (low P/B, low P/E, high yield), so it excludes US names by design and leans Europe and Japan.
The character of the resulting portfolio — value screens overseas concentrate it in European banks, energy, telecoms and Japanese industrials, giving a cyclical, financials-heavy personality.
The nature of its income — a structurally high dividend yield paid in foreign currencies and subject to withholding, so a meaningful part of total return arrives as taxed, FX-exposed income.
greenFlags: Holdings P/B and yield genuinely cheaper than the MSCI EAFE parent (EFV, IVLU) — real cross-border value, not a relabeled EAFE blend.
A profitability or balance-sheet screen layered on cheapness — avoids the European value traps that perennially screen cheap for a reason.
Currency exposure left deliberately unhedged and disclosed as such — value rotations overseas often coincide with a weakening dollar, which adds to USD returns.
redFlags: Sector and country weights nearly matching plain EAFE — value in name only, won't pay off when value rotates.
A few perpetually cheap megabanks or auto names dominating the top 10 — classic foreign value traps, cheap because the franchise is impaired.
Lagging MSCI EAFE Value by >100 bps annualised over 5Y — a wrong-basket or FX-drag failure beyond value simply being out of favour.

mostImportant: How it selects — large-cap developed-market stocks outside the US screened on growth traits (earnings/sales momentum, high reinvestment), so it tilts toward European luxury, semis, healthcare and industrials.

The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated in a handful of high-multiple 'global champions' (luxury, ASML-type semis, pharma), making it more concentrated and momentum-sensitive than foreign blend.
The nature of its income — a structurally low dividend yield, so most of the expected return must come from price appreciation and currency rather than distributions.
greenFlags: Reinvestment runway evidence in holdings (rising ROIC, high R&D intensity) rather than just past price momentum — durable compounders (e.g. IDMO/EFG constituents), not a chase list.
Multi-year EAFE-Growth outperformance that survives a momentum reversal — shows stock selection, not just a factor tailwind.
Disciplined single-name caps near ~5% — prevents one luxury or semi name from becoming an undiversified bet.
redFlags: Top-10 weight past ~40% in a few mega-cap exporters — a concentrated bet on a couple of global franchises masquerading as diversified.
High portfolio multiple with decelerating holdings-level revenue growth — paying growth prices for stalling businesses.
Turnover above ~60% from chasing price momentum — taxable churn that erodes the thin yield this category already offers.

mostImportant: What it tracks — large- and mid-cap European developed-market equities (often MSCI/STOXX Europe), so the portfolio is built from UK, French, Swiss, German and Nordic names.

The character of the resulting portfolio — heavy in financials, healthcare, luxury/consumer staples and industrials, with a cluster of multinational exporters whose earnings depend on demand outside Europe.
The nature of its income — a higher dividend yield than the US market, paid in EUR/GBP/CHF and subject to per-country withholding, so currency moves and tax treaties materially shape net income.
greenFlags: Disclosed currency policy that matches the buyer's intent — a hedged share class (e.g. HEDJ) for those wanting pure equity beta, unhedged (VGK/IEV) for those who want the euro exposure.
Country caps that prevent the UK or France from dominating — keeps it a Europe fund, not a closet single-country bet.
Reclaim of treaty withholding on Swiss/French dividends — recovers yield that less diligent funds leave on the table.
redFlags: An outsized weight in one or two megabanks plus one luxury/semi name — concentration risk that the broad 'Europe' label disguises.
Eurozone-only construction sold as 'Europe' — silently drops the UK and Switzerland, changing sector and currency exposure.
A premium to NAV during US hours after European exchanges close — intraday pricing on stale local marks.

mostImportant: What it tracks — Japanese large- and mid-cap equities (TOPIX or MSCI Japan), so the basket is dominated by autos, industrials, electronics, trading houses and megabanks.
The character of the resulting portfolio — highly cyclical and export-sensitive, with returns swinging on the yen, BOJ policy and the pace of corporate-governance reform.
The nature of its income and tax — moderate dividends plus a rising trend of buybacks, paid in yen and subject to Japanese withholding, so yen direction often dominates the USD total return.
greenFlags: An explicit currency choice that's disclosed — a yen-hedged class (DXJ) for those expecting a weak yen, unhedged (EWJ/BBJP) for those wanting yen upside, rather than an unstated default.
Tilt toward firms raising payout ratios and unwinding cross-shareholdings — captures the governance-reform catalyst that's specific to Japan right now.
Broad TOPIX-style breadth rather than Nikkei price-weighting — avoids the distortion where a few high-priced shares drive a price-weighted index.
redFlags: Unhedged exposure marketed on Japan's earnings recovery — a strengthening yen can wipe out local equity gains in USD terms.
Heavy concentration in a single trading house or automaker — one keiretsu name carrying the fund's performance.
A persistent premium to NAV in US trading — Tokyo is closed during US hours, so the intraday price rests on stale marks.

mostImportant: What it covers — small- and mid-cap stocks worldwide including the US, so it reaches the long tail of companies that large-cap world indexes miss.

The character of the resulting portfolio — far more domestically driven and economically sensitive than global large-cap, with thousands of holdings and lower single-name concentration but higher volatility.
The nature of its income — a modest, mixed-currency yield, with returns expected to come mostly from price appreciation across many small positions rather than distributions.
greenFlags: Genuine global small/mid breadth with hundreds-to-thousands of holdings and low top-10 weight (e.g. VSS-style) — diversification doing the work small caps need.
Profitability screening that excludes perennially loss-making micro-caps — filters the junk that drags naive small-cap indexes.
Tight bid-ask spreads and disclosed fair-value pricing — confirms the manager can trade an illiquid global tail without large transaction drag.
redFlags: A wide, persistent premium/discount to NAV — small-cap baskets are illiquid, so the market price can detach from holdings value.
Liquidity concentrated in a few large holdings while the tail barely trades — redemptions could force fire-sales of the illiquid names.
Annual turnover above ~50% — small/mid rebalancing churn with wide spreads quietly compounds into real lost return.

mostImportant: How it selects — large-cap stocks worldwide including the US, screened on value traits, so it blends cheap US financials/energy with cheap European and Japanese cyclicals.

The character of the resulting portfolio — a financials-, energy- and industrials-heavy global cyclical tilt whose US share is lower than in a global blend fund because US large-caps screen 'growth' more often.
The nature of its income — a structurally high, multi-currency dividend yield, so a large share of expected return arrives as income subject to a mix of qualified and withheld treatment.
greenFlags: Holdings genuinely cheaper than the MSCI ACWI parent on P/B and P/E — real worldwide value, not an ACWI clone with a value label.
A quality screen that keeps zombie cyclicals out — avoids the global value traps concentrated in banks and commodities.
Foreign tax credit passed through on the ex-US sleeve — recovers withholding on the large foreign dividend stream this style generates.
redFlags: Sector and regional weights mirroring global blend — value in name only, no payoff when the value factor rotates.
Over-reliance on a few cheap megabanks and oil majors across regions — correlated value traps that move together in a downturn.
Lagging MSCI ACWI Value by >100 bps annualised over 5Y — a wrong-basket failure distinct from value being out of favour.

mostImportant: What it covers — developed and some emerging Asia-Pacific markets EXCLUDING Japan (Australia, Hong Kong, Singapore, Korea, Taiwan), so it carves out the region's most diverse mix of economies.

The character of the resulting portfolio — dominated by Australian and Korean/Taiwanese banks, miners and semiconductors, making it a leveraged play on commodities, China demand and the global chip cycle.
The nature of its income — a relatively high dividend yield (driven by Australian financials and Taiwanese tech) paid in multiple currencies and subject to varying withholding rates.
greenFlags: Country caps that balance Australia against Korea/Taiwan — prevents the fund from becoming an Australian-bank or a single-chipmaker bet.
Physical replication across the liquid names (e.g. EPP-style) rather than swaps for the harder markets — you hold the stocks, not derivative access.
Disclosed treatment of dual-listed China exposure (Hong Kong H-shares) — clarity on how much China sneaks in via the 'ex-Japan' label.
redFlags: A single semiconductor name carrying an outsized weight — what looks regional is really a concentrated chip-cycle wager.
Heavy unhedged Australian-dollar exposure undisclosed as a driver — AUD swings with commodities can dominate the equity return.
Premium to NAV during US hours when all these exchanges are closed — intraday pricing on stale Asia-Pacific marks.

mostImportant: How it selects — large-cap stocks worldwide including the US, screened on growth traits, so US mega-cap tech plus a few European/Asian champions dominate the basket.

The character of the resulting portfolio — the most concentrated and momentum-sensitive of the global blend variants, with the 'Magnificent Seven' plus names like ASML and luxury leaders carrying outsized weight.
The nature of its income — a low dividend yield, so nearly all expected return depends on price appreciation and currency rather than distributions.
greenFlags: Holdings-level reinvestment evidence (rising ROIC, high R&D) across regions — durable global compounders rather than a momentum chase.
Single-name caps that keep US mega-cap tech from swamping the ex-US growth sleeve — preserves the 'global' character.
Outperformance of MSCI ACWI Growth that persists through a momentum reversal — stock selection, not just factor beta.
redFlags: Top-10 weight past ~45% in US mega-cap tech — a concentrated US growth bet wearing a global label.
Rich portfolio multiple with decelerating holdings-level growth — paying up for names whose fundamentals are slowing.
Turnover above ~60% from price-momentum chasing — taxable churn that erodes an already minimal yield.

mostImportant: How it selects — small- and mid-cap developed-market stocks OUTSIDE the US screened on value traits, reaching the cheapest, least-covered tail of the international market.

The character of the resulting portfolio — domestically driven European and Japanese small-caps in industrials, materials and financials, with thin analyst coverage and high country/currency sensitivity.
The nature of its income — a high dividend yield from cheap, mature small-caps, paid in foreign currencies and subject to withholding, so net yield depends heavily on FX and treaty reclaims.
greenFlags: Holdings genuinely cheaper than the foreign small/mid parent on P/B with a profitability filter (e.g. DLS-style) — captures the value premium where it's strongest while screening out distress.
Hundreds of holdings with low single-name weight — the diversification illiquid foreign small-caps require to be ownable.
Disclosed fair-value pricing and tight tracking — shows the manager can value a thin, multi-time-zone basket accurately.
redFlags: A persistent discount/premium to NAV — foreign small-cap baskets are illiquid and trade away from holdings value.
Cheap, deteriorating micro-caps concentrated by country — value traps in a single weak economy, not broad mispricing.
Turnover above ~50% across wide-spread foreign small-caps — transaction drag that quietly consumes the value edge.

mostImportant: What it covers — small- and mid-cap developed-market stocks outside the US across value and growth, the international counterpart to a US extended-market or small-cap blend fund.

The character of the resulting portfolio — thousands of domestically focused European, Japanese and Pacific names, far more diversified by company but far more exposed to local economies and currencies than foreign large-cap.
The nature of its income — a modest, multi-currency yield, with returns expected to come mainly from broad price appreciation across the small-cap tail.
greenFlags: Comprehensive breadth with low top-10 weight (e.g. SCZ/VSS-style) — owns the full foreign small/mid universe rather than a liquid subset.
A quality/profitability screen on the small-cap tail — removes perennial loss-makers that drag naive cap-weighted small-cap indexes.
Tight spreads and a small, stable premium to NAV — evidence the structure handles illiquid international small-caps cleanly.
redFlags: Heavy country concentration (e.g. Japan or UK well over its parent weight) — a regional bet hidden inside a 'foreign' label.
A wide, swinging premium/discount to NAV — the small-cap basket is too illiquid for arbitrage to keep price near value.
Liquidity clustered in a few large holdings while the tail barely trades — redemption pressure could force selling the liquid names and skewing the portfolio.

mostImportant: What it covers — the whole Pacific/Asia region INCLUDING Japan plus Australia, Hong Kong, Singapore and often Korea/Taiwan, so Japan is usually the single largest country weight.

The character of the resulting portfolio — a Japan-plus-Australia core with industrials, financials, autos and tech, blending a developed-Japan anchor with commodity- and China-linked Pacific exposure.
The nature of its income — a moderate, multi-currency yield with returns split between Japanese earnings/governance trends and Australian/Asian commodity and rate cycles.
greenFlags: Country caps that keep Japan from overwhelming the rest — keeps it diversified rather than a closet Japan fund with an Asian sleeve.
Disclosed currency policy across yen and Aussie dollar — clarity on whether FX is a hedged or intended return driver.
Physical replication of the liquid Pacific names — direct ownership instead of swap-based access to harder markets.
redFlags: Japan weight so high the fund effectively IS a Japan fund — buyers get little incremental Pacific diversification.
Concentration in one Australian bank/miner or one Asian chipmaker — single-name risk under a 'diversified' banner.
Premium to NAV during US hours when every constituent exchange is closed — intraday pricing on stale Asia-Pacific marks.

mostImportant: How it selects — small- and mid-cap developed-market stocks outside the US screened on growth traits, reaching fast-growing, under-covered international companies.

The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated in European and Asian niche industrials, healthcare and tech with high multiples, thin coverage and elevated volatility versus foreign large growth.
The nature of its income — a very low dividend yield, so essentially all expected return must come from price appreciation and currency.
greenFlags: Holdings with rising revenue and reinvestment runway, not just past price momentum — genuine emerging compounders in the international small-cap tail.
Broad diversification (hundreds of names, low single-name weight) — spreads the high idiosyncratic risk of foreign small-cap growth.
A profitability gate that excludes cash-burning micro-caps — keeps speculative loss-makers out of a momentum-prone style.
redFlags: Top holdings driven by price momentum with decelerating fundamentals — a chase list dressed as a growth portfolio.
Turnover above ~60% across wide-spread foreign small-caps — transaction drag that overwhelms a near-zero yield.
A swinging premium/discount to NAV — illiquid international small-cap growth names that trade away from holdings value.

mostImportant: What it tracks — a total-market index covering essentially the entire investable equity universe of its home country (large, mid and small caps in one cap-weighted basket, e.g. a Canadian or US total-market index).

The character of the resulting portfolio — cap weighting means the largest companies still dominate, so a 'total market' Canadian fund is effectively a big bet on banks and energy, and a US one on mega-cap tech.
The nature of its income — broadly market-level dividends, mostly eligible/qualified, with the small-cap tail adding breadth but little extra yield.
greenFlags: Thousands of holdings with the small/mid tail genuinely represented — true total-market breadth (e.g. VCN in Canada, VTI in the US), not a large-cap index relabeled.
Securities-lending income rebated to the fund — can pull net cost below the headline fee and tighten tracking.
Near-zero capital-gains distributions over 5Y — in-kind redemption discipline that compounds in a taxable account.
redFlags: Top-10 weight far above the broad market's (in Canada, the big banks plus a couple of energy names) — 'total market' that's quietly a concentrated sector bet.
Tracking drift above ~25 bps versus the named total-market index — usually basket drift or an expired waiver, and it's pure lost return.
A mid-life benchmark switch or widened sampling that thins the small-cap tail — the basket no longer matches the total-market name.

mostImportant: What it tracks — the large-capitalization segment of its home market via a cap-weighted index, deliberately excluding the mid- and small-cap tail.
The character of the resulting portfolio — dominated by a handful of mega-caps and their sectors, so a Canadian large-cap fund leans on banks and energy while a US one leans on mega-cap tech.
The nature of its income — large-caps pay the bulk of the market's dividends, mostly eligible/qualified, so yield is steady and tax-favoured.
greenFlags: Holdings that match the large-cap index cleanly without creeping into mid-caps — true large-cap exposure (e.g. XIU-style in Canada, IVV/VV in the US).
Securities-lending income rebated back — quietly lowers net cost and helps track at or just above the index.
Low reconstitution turnover — fewer forced taxable trades than rules-heavy large-cap screens.
redFlags: Top-10 weight past the index's already-high concentration — a 'large-cap' fund that's really a few-name bet (banks in Canada, mega-cap tech in the US).
A narrow constituent count (e.g. a 60-stock Canadian index) sold as broad — far less diversified than the name implies.
Tracking drift above ~25 bps from an expired waiver or sampling error — direct lost return on an index that should be cheap to replicate.

mostImportant: What it tracks — broad US-listed equity exposure (often a total-market or S&P-500-style index) packaged for a non-US (frequently Canadian) investor, so the underlying is the US market.
The character of the resulting portfolio — dominated by US mega-cap tech and the sectors around it, with returns driven as much by the USD/CAD exchange rate as by the stocks for an unhedged buyer.
The nature of its income — mostly qualified US dividends subject to US withholding, whose recoverability depends on the holding account (e.g. RRSP vs taxable) and the fund's structure.
greenFlags: A clearly labeled currency choice — a CAD-hedged class (e.g. VSP/XSP) for those who want pure US equity, unhedged (VFV/VUN) for those who want USD exposure, rather than an ambiguous default.
US-domiciled-ETF wrapping that avoids a second layer of withholding in tax-sheltered accounts — preserves more of the dividend.
Tracking the US index within ~15 bps after hedging costs — confirms the hedge overlay isn't quietly bleeding return.
redFlags: A 'wrap of a wrap' (Canadian ETF holding a US ETF holding the stocks) adding a hidden fee and withholding layer — quietly raises cost and reduces yield.
A currency hedge whose roll cost runs well above the rate differential — drag that erodes the very protection it's sold for.
Marketed as 'US equity' but built on a narrow index — buyers expecting broad US exposure get concentrated large-cap instead.

mostImportant: How it selects — screens the market for above-average dividend yield, then cap- or yield-weights the survivors, so the index is a rules-based income tilt rather than a broad-market fund.

The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated in financials, utilities, energy, telecoms and staples, giving it a defensive, rate-sensitive, value-leaning personality versus the broad market.
The nature of its income — a structurally higher dividend yield, so a large share of total return arrives as income, taxed as eligible/qualified dividends where applicable.
greenFlags: A dividend-quality or sustainability screen (payout coverage, balance sheet) layered on raw yield (e.g. VYM, SCHD) — filters out the high-yield-because-the-stock-fell names.
Multi-year consecutive dividend GROWTH in the holdings — durable payout health rather than reaching for the highest current yield.
Sector caps that prevent the screen from piling into utilities and financials — keeps it diversified and less rate-sensitive than a pure yield sort.
redFlags: Top names whose yield is high because the price collapsed — yield-trap exposure that screens cheap right before a dividend cut.
Heavy single-sector concentration (utilities plus REITs plus telecoms) — a rate bet dressed as an income strategy, vulnerable when yields rise.
Payout ratios in the holdings above sustainable levels — current yield that the underlying earnings can't fund, signaling future cuts.

mostImportant: What it tracks — the small-capitalization segment of its home market via a rules-based index, so company size, not sector, defines the basket.

The character of the resulting portfolio — domestically driven, economically sensitive and more volatile than large-cap, with heavier weights in industrials, materials, financials and (in Canada) resources.
The nature of its income — a modest dividend yield, since returns are expected mainly from price appreciation across many smaller, faster-changing companies.
greenFlags: A profitability or quality screen on the small-cap universe (e.g. IJR/AVUV-style) — excludes the perennial loss-makers that drag naive cap-weighted small-cap indexes.
Hundreds of holdings with low single-name weight — the diversification small-caps need to be ownable.
Tight tracking and a small, stable premium to NAV — evidence the manager can trade an illiquid small-cap basket without large transaction drag.
redFlags: A wide, persistent premium/discount to NAV — small-cap baskets are illiquid and the market price can detach from holdings value.
Liquidity concentrated in a few large holdings while the tail barely trades — redemptions could force fire-sales of the thin names.
Annual turnover above ~50% from index reconstitution — small-cap churn with wide spreads quietly compounds into lost return.

mostImportant: What it tracks — the mid-capitalization band between large and small caps via a rules-based index, capturing companies past the start-up stage but below mega-cap scale.
The character of the resulting portfolio — more cyclical and less mega-cap-dominated than a large-cap fund, with a sector mix tilted toward industrials, financials and consumer/real-estate names.
The nature of its income — a moderate dividend yield between large- and small-cap levels, with appreciation expected to drive most of the return.
greenFlags: A clean mid-cap band that doesn't bleed into mega-caps at the top or micro-caps at the bottom — true mid-cap exposure (e.g. IJH/VO-style), not a watered-down large-cap proxy.
Securities-lending income rebated to the fund — helps offset the slightly higher cost of trading mid-cap names.
Modest turnover despite migration in and out of the band — disciplined handling of the size boundaries that cause forced trades.
redFlags: Heavy overlap with the large-cap index at the top end — investors double-pay for exposure they already hold in a large-cap fund.
Tracking drift above ~25 bps from boundary churn — names crossing the size threshold force taxable trades and lost return.
A thin constituent count concentrated in a few sectors — a 'mid-cap' fund that's really a narrow cyclical bet.

mostImportant: What it tracks — the 'completion' index of everything OUTSIDE the large-cap benchmark (the mid-, small- and micro-cap tail), designed to be paired with an S&P 500 fund to recreate the total market.
The character of the resulting portfolio — explicitly excludes the mega-caps, so it has no S&P 500 overlap and is more volatile, more cyclical and more domestically driven than a total-market fund.
The nature of its income — a low dividend yield, with return expected almost entirely from appreciation across thousands of smaller holdings.
greenFlags: Clean exclusion of the large-cap benchmark with no S&P 500 overlap (e.g. VXF) — does its completion job precisely when paired with a 500 fund.
Broad holdings count reaching into micro-caps — captures the full tail the large-cap index leaves out, not just mid-caps.
In-kind redemption keeping capital-gains distributions near zero — important because the small/micro tail would otherwise generate taxable trades.
redFlags: Creeping large-cap overlap as held names graduate in size — erodes the completion mandate and double-counts exposure for paired holders.
A wide premium/discount to NAV — the micro-cap tail is illiquid and price can detach from holdings value.
Turnover spikes when names migrate into the large-cap index and must be sold — forced taxable trades at the size boundary.

mostImportant: What it tracks — a broad, rules-based market index spanning most of the investable universe (often a total-market or broad-composite index), positioned as a one-fund core holding.

The character of the resulting portfolio — cap-weighted breadth that still leans on the largest names and their sectors, so the 'broad' label hides a top-heavy tilt (banks/energy in Canada, mega-cap tech in the US).
The nature of its income — market-level dividends, mostly eligible/qualified, with the breadth adding diversification more than incremental yield.
greenFlags: Genuine all-cap breadth with the small/mid tail represented — a true broad-market core (e.g. ITOT/VTI, or VCN in Canada), not a large-cap index relabeled 'broad'.
Securities-lending income rebated to the fund — pulls net cost below the headline fee and tightens tracking.
Near-zero realised capital-gains distributions over 5Y — in-kind redemption discipline that compounds in a taxable account.
redFlags: Top-10 weight well above the underlying market's — a 'broad' fund that's quietly a concentrated mega-cap or sector bet.
Tracking drift above ~25 bps from sampling or an expired waiver — pure lost return on an index meant to be cheap to replicate.
A mid-life benchmark or methodology change that narrows coverage — the basket no longer matches the broad-market name you bought.

mostImportant: How the index defines the sleeve — a rules-based basket spanning energy, metals & mining, agriculture and timber/paper equities, so the mix of upstream commodity producers vs. downstream processors drives behaviour.
The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated, cyclical and inflation-sensitive, with returns tied to underlying commodity prices and global capex cycles rather than broad-market earnings.
The nature of its income — distributions come largely from cash-generative producers and can be lumpy, swinging with commodity-driven payout and buyback cycles.
greenFlags: Spread across energy, metals and agriculture rather than a single commodity (GUNR/FTRI) — diversifies the sub-sector booms and busts that sink narrow resource funds.
Tilt toward integrated, low-cost producers with strong reserves — survives the trough of the commodity cycle when high-cost marginal names get wiped out.
Includes upstream resource owners (royalties, reserves, timberland) — captures the price upside rather than just margin-squeezed refiners and processors.
redFlags: Single-commodity concentration hidden under a broad 'natural resources' label — really an oil or copper bet that ignores the diversification the name implies.
Heavy weight in high-cost marginal producers — first to cut payouts and bleed cash when prices roll over, amplifying the downcycle.
Lagging a broad resources benchmark by >150 bps annualised through a full cycle — usually a wrong-basket tilt to laggard sub-sectors, not just commodities being out of favour.

mostImportant: How the index scopes 'industrials' — a rules-based sector basket of capital goods, aerospace & defense, machinery, transports and commercial services, cap-weighted within the sector.
The character of the resulting portfolio — economically cyclical and capex-driven, so it leads early in recoveries and lags into slowdowns, with a heavy mega-cap aerospace/machinery tilt.
The nature of its income — moderate dividend yield from mature manufacturers, mostly qualified, with payouts that track the industrial capex cycle.
greenFlags: Balanced across aerospace/defense, machinery and transports rather than top-heavy in a few mega-caps (VIS/XLI) — avoids being a hidden bet on one or two names.
Meaningful aerospace & defense weight with order-backlog visibility — provides a counter-cyclical anchor when broad capex stalls.
Exposure to automation and electrification capital goods — captures the structural reshoring/efficiency spend, not just old-economy heavy machinery.
redFlags: Top-10 weight past ~45% in a handful of mega-cap industrials — sector beta in name, single-stock risk in practice.
Overweight late-cycle transports and freight just as PMIs roll over — the most cyclical corner, first to de-rate in a slowdown.
A benchmark switch that quietly folds in services or conglomerates — dilutes the industrial-cyclical exposure the name promised.

mostImportant: How the index scopes the sleeve — a rules-based basket of banks, insurers, capital-markets firms and diversified financials, cap-weighted within the sector (serves Canada's 'Financials' label too).

The character of the resulting portfolio — rate-sensitive and balance-sheet-driven, with returns hinging on the yield curve, credit cycle and regulatory capital rules; in Canada, dominated by a few large national banks.
The nature of its income — a structurally higher dividend yield than the broad market, so more total return arrives as income, much of it qualified.
greenFlags: Spread across banks, insurers and capital-markets firms (XLF/VFH) — insurance and exchanges offset the credit-cycle risk that pure-bank funds carry.
Quality screen favouring well-capitalised lenders with strong CET1 ratios — survives credit shocks that impair thinly-capitalised peers.
Capital-markets and asset-manager weight that earns fee income — diversifies away from pure net-interest-margin dependence on the yield curve.
redFlags: Heavy regional-bank concentration — deposit-flight and duration-mismatch risk, as the 2023 SVB-driven regional rout showed.
Top-5 weight past ~55% in a few national banks (common in Canada-style baskets) — sector label masking a near-single-stock credit bet.
Outsized exposure to a stressed credit niche (CRE, consumer subprime) — concentrated loss vector that doesn't show up in the sector name.

mostImportant: How the index scopes 'energy' — a rules-based basket of oil & gas producers, refiners, services and (increasingly) some clean-energy names, cap-weighted and historically dominated by integrated majors (serves Canada's 'Energy' label).
The character of the resulting portfolio — highly concentrated, commodity-price-driven and volatile, with returns tracking crude/gas spot and global supply discipline rather than broad earnings.
The nature of its income — high, cash-flow-funded dividends from the majors, often supplemented by variable buybacks that swing with the oil price.
greenFlags: Tilt to integrated majors and low-breakeven producers (XLE/VDE) — generate free cash and sustain payouts even when crude trades near marginal cost.
Includes midstream/infrastructure alongside E&P — toll-like cash flows dampen the swings of pure upstream price exposure.
Capital-discipline tilt favouring shareholder returns over drilling growth — the post-2020 shift that rewards balance-sheet strength over production volume.
redFlags: Concentrated in high-cost shale or small-cap E&P — cash-burn and solvency risk the moment crude drops toward breakeven.
Heavy oilfield-services weight — the most operationally levered, cyclical corner, first to cut payouts when capex budgets freeze.
Lagging a broad energy benchmark by >150 bps annualised through a cycle — a wrong-basket sub-sector tilt, distinct from oil simply being weak.

mostImportant: What lands here — narrow, hard-to-classify single-sector or niche-industry funds (e.g. gaming, cannabis, water, space) that don't fit a standard GICS sleeve, so the index rules vary wildly by product.
The character of the resulting portfolio — typically concentrated, often small/mid-cap and illiquid, with a bespoke selection methodology that needs to be read directly rather than assumed.
The nature of its income — usually low or no dividend yield, since these niches skew toward growth or pre-profit thematic names.
greenFlags: A transparent, rules-based screen with clear inclusion criteria — avoids the manager-discretion drift that plagues novelty niche funds.
Liquidity-weighted or float-adjusted construction rather than equal-weight on micro-caps — limits the impact-cost bleed when the fund rebalances.
Sufficient AUM and tight bid/ask spreads relative to peers — a niche fund that won't trade at large premiums or face closure risk.
redFlags: Tiny AUM (well under ~$50M) with wide spreads — closure/delisting risk and costly entry/exit in a thin niche.
Equal-weighting illiquid micro-caps — forced rebalance trades move the very names it holds, leaking return to market impact.
A vague or frequently-revised methodology — a 'theme' chasing a fad rather than a durable, investable basket.

mostImportant: How the index defines the universe — a rules-based basket of China-region equities that may mix mainland A-shares, Hong Kong H-shares, ADRs and sometimes Taiwan, so share-class scope drives what you actually own.
The character of the resulting portfolio — single-country/region concentrated, heavily tech-and-internet-weighted, and exposed to state policy, VIE legal structures and ADR-delisting risk.
The nature of its income — modest dividends, often subject to foreign withholding, with total return dominated by price and currency (CNY/HKD) moves.
greenFlags: Broad share-class coverage spanning A-shares, H-shares and ADRs (MCHI/FXI vs. narrow ADR-only funds) — captures the full opportunity set and reduces single-venue delisting risk.
Caps on the largest internet names — limits the single-stock policy-shock risk that concentrated China funds carry.
Direct A-share access via Stock Connect rather than ADR-only — sidesteps the VIE-structure and US-delisting overhang on offshore listings.
redFlags: ADR-only construction with heavy VIE exposure — a US-delisting or audit-access dispute can force a costly forced sale at depressed prices.
Top-5 weight dominated by two or three internet mega-caps — a regulatory crackdown on one name craters the whole fund.
Unhedged share-class arbitrage between A and H lines — persistent NAV dislocations and tracking drift versus the stated benchmark.

mostImportant: How the index scopes the sleeve — a rules-based basket of discretionary spending names: autos, retail, apparel, restaurants, travel and homebuilders, cap-weighted within the sector (serves Canada's 'Consumer Discretionary' label).
The character of the resulting portfolio — economically sensitive and consumer-spending-driven, typically top-heavy in one or two e-commerce/auto mega-caps that dominate the cap-weighting.
The nature of its income — a low dividend yield, since the sector reinvests for growth, so total return is overwhelmingly price-driven.
greenFlags: Spread across retail, autos, leisure and homebuilders rather than dominated by one e-commerce giant (XLY's Amazon/Tesla concentration is the cautionary case) — true sector exposure, not a two-stock proxy.
Tilt toward off-price and value retail with pricing power — holds up through consumer downturns when discretionary spend contracts.
Balanced goods-vs-services discretionary mix — captures the post-pandemic rotation into experiences rather than just durable goods.
redFlags: Top-2 weight past ~40% in a couple of mega-caps — sector beta that's really an idiosyncratic bet on Amazon/Tesla-type names.
Overweight big-ticket durables (autos, furniture) heading into a credit tightening — the first discretionary spend consumers defer.
Heavy mall-based or fashion-cyclical retail — structurally challenged names that lag even when the broad consumer is healthy.

mostImportant: How the index scopes the sleeve — a rules-based basket blending legacy telecom/media with interactive-media and internet platforms after the 2018 GICS reshuffle, cap-weighted (serves Canada's 'Communication Services' label).
The character of the resulting portfolio — barbelled between a few mega-cap internet/advertising platforms and slower-growth telecom incumbents, so the top names dominate behaviour.
The nature of its income — bifurcated: high-yield telecoms supply most of the dividends while the internet platforms pay little, so income concentration sits in the legacy names.
greenFlags: Caps on the largest platform names so the basket isn't two mega-caps (XLC's Meta/Alphabet weight is the cautionary case) — keeps it a sector fund, not a duopoly bet.
Genuine telecom and media diversification alongside the platforms — the dividend-paying incumbents cushion advertising-cycle drawdowns.
Exposure to interactive media beyond search/social (gaming, streaming) — broadens the growth engine past the ad-dependent giants.
redFlags: Top-2 weight past ~40% in two internet platforms — an ad-recession or antitrust action on one name sinks the fund.
Heavy weight in over-levered legacy telecom — high yields masking secular decline and dividend-cut risk.
A post-GICS benchmark that still leans old-economy telecom — misses the interactive-media growth the modern sector name implies.

mostImportant: What the fund actually holds — equities of crypto-exposed businesses (miners, exchanges, treasury-holding companies) and/or spot/futures crypto vehicles, so the structure ranges from operating-company equity to direct coin exposure.

The character of the resulting portfolio — extremely concentrated and high-beta to bitcoin/crypto prices, with returns amplified versus the underlying coins through operating and balance-sheet leverage.
The nature of its income — typically none; total return is pure price, and for Canada-listed spot products the wrapper, custody and currency-hedge mechanics define the exposure.
greenFlags: For Canada spot products, qualified cold-storage custody with tight premium/discount to NAV (BTCC/EBIT) — you get clean coin exposure without paying a structural NAV markup.
A management fee well below buying and self-custodying the asset, with that fee disclosed against in-kind creation — minimal drag versus holding the coin directly.
Clear hedge mechanics on currency-hedged classes (the 'Short CAD' leg hedges CAD back to USD exposure) — removes FX noise so the fund tracks the coin, not the loonie.
redFlags: Persistent premium/discount to NAV beyond a few percent — you're buying or selling the wrapper, not the asset, and the gap can snap against you.
Equity baskets dominated by one treasury-strategy proxy (MSTR-type names) — layered balance-sheet leverage that can magnify drawdowns far beyond the coin itself.
Custody or counterparty opacity on a synthetic/futures-based structure — roll cost and basis risk silently erode tracking versus spot.

mostImportant: How the index defines 'infrastructure' — a rules-based basket of utilities, pipelines, toll roads, airports, towers and other long-lived hard assets, so the utility-vs-transport-vs-energy-midstream mix shapes the risk.
The character of the resulting portfolio — lower-beta, cash-flow-stable and rate-sensitive, with regulated/contracted revenues that often carry inflation linkage.
The nature of its income — a structurally high, contractually-supported dividend yield, so a large share of total return arrives as income.
greenFlags: Genuine spread across utilities, transport and midstream rather than a relabeled utilities fund (IGF/NFRA) — diversifies regulatory and commodity exposures.
Holdings with explicit CPI-linked or regulated tariff escalators — real inflation pass-through, the core reason to own the asset class.
Concession/contracted-asset weight (toll roads, airports, towers) — long-duration cash flows that hold up when rates and the economy wobble.
redFlags: Effectively a utilities fund wearing an 'infrastructure' label — pure rate-duration risk without the diversification the name implies.
Heavy unregulated power or merchant-generation exposure — commodity-price risk that undercuts the stable-cash-flow thesis.
Elevated leverage across holdings into a rising-rate regime — refinancing pressure that threatens the very distributions the fund is bought for.

mostImportant: What the fund holds and how it's wrapped — primarily midstream MLPs (pipelines, storage, processing); a >25% MLP allocation forces a C-corp structure that pays entity-level tax, so the wrapper choice is central.

The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated in a handful of large midstream names with toll-like, fee-based cash flows, less commodity-sensitive than upstream energy but still energy-linked.
The nature of its income — very high distribution yield, often part return-of-capital, with the C-corp/RIC structure determining whether you receive a K-1 or 1099 and how it's taxed.
greenFlags: RIC-structured (1099, capped MLP weight) for tax-deferred accounts vs. C-corp for taxable — matching the wrapper to the account avoids the deferred-tax-liability drag (AMLP's C-corp drag is the cautionary case).
Fee-based, volume-contracted midstream rather than commodity-spread processors — stable distribution coverage through the oil cycle.
Strong distribution coverage ratios across holdings (cash flow comfortably above payout) — durable yield rather than a payout funded by new debt or equity.
redFlags: A C-corp structure accruing a deferred tax liability that silently widens NAV tracking drag versus the underlying index — a hidden, compounding cost unique to this wrapper.
Top-heavy concentration in two or three midstream giants — a single distribution cut or counterparty failure craters the fund's income.
Distributions heavily reliant on return-of-capital with thin coverage — yield that erodes the NAV base rather than reflecting earnings.

mostImportant: What the fund holds — equities of gold/silver miners (and sometimes streamers/royalties), not the metal itself, so returns are levered to the metal price through mining operating margins.
The character of the resulting portfolio — concentrated, high-beta and operationally levered to gold/silver spot, amplifying both rallies and drawdowns versus the underlying metal.
The nature of its income — generally low yield; total return is dominated by metal-price moves and mine-level cost and execution risk.
greenFlags: Tilt to senior, low-all-in-sustaining-cost producers (GDX vs. GDXJ's junior risk) — margins survive when the metal price falls toward marginal cost.
Meaningful royalty/streaming weight (Franco-Nevada, Wheaton-type names) — captures metal-price upside without mine-level cost inflation and execution risk.
Geographic diversification across stable jurisdictions — limits the resource-nationalism and permitting shocks that hit single-country miners.
redFlags: Heavy junior/explorer weight — financing and execution risk that turns into permanent capital loss in a weak-metal tape, beyond the gold price itself.
Concentration in miners operating in high-political-risk jurisdictions — expropriation or permit-revocation tail risk the metal price doesn't carry.
Holdings with rising all-in sustaining costs eroding margins even as gold holds — operational decay masked by a flat metal price.

mostImportant: How the index defines the universe — a rules-based single-country basket of Indian equities, accessed via local listings or ADRs, with FPI rules and access-vehicle structure shaping what's actually held.
The character of the resulting portfolio — single-country concentrated, heavily weighted to financials, IT services and consumer names, and exposed to INR currency and domestic-policy risk.
The nature of its income — low dividend yield; total return is dominated by price and the rupee, with a structurally high domestic valuation premium baked in.
greenFlags: Broad large-and-mid-cap coverage rather than a handful of ADRs (INDA/EPI) — captures the domestic growth story, not just the offshore-listed minority.
Caps on the largest financials/conglomerates — limits single-group concentration risk (Adani/Reliance-style episodes are the cautionary case).
Transparent, fully-invested replication with low cash drag — avoids the access-fee and P-note structure that can decouple a fund from the index.
redFlags: ADR-heavy or P-note-based construction — structural access risk and tracking drift versus the local-market index.
Concentration in a single business group across multiple holdings — a governance or leverage shock to one promoter group hits the whole fund.
Buying at a stretched domestic valuation premium with thin earnings support — high re-rating risk distinct from the long-term growth thesis.

mostImportant: How the index scopes the sleeve — a rules-based global basket of listed REITs and property companies spanning regions and property types (residential, office, retail, industrial, data centers), cap-weighted.
The character of the resulting portfolio — rate-sensitive and yield-oriented, with returns driven by cap rates, property-type cycles and, for non-US holdings, foreign currency.
The nature of its income — high distribution yield from rental cash flows, much of it ordinary (non-qualified) income, so the tax character matters in taxable accounts.
greenFlags: Diversified across regions and property types rather than concentrated in one (REET/VNQI) — spreads the divergent office, retail and logistics cycles.
Weight toward secular-growth property types (data centers, logistics, towers) — structural demand that offsets the decline in legacy office and mall assets.
Holdings with conservative balance sheets and laddered debt maturities — survive a refinancing squeeze when cap rates rise.
redFlags: Heavy office and traditional-retail concentration — structurally impaired property types that lag even as the broad sector recovers.
Elevated portfolio-level leverage into a rising-rate regime — refinancing pressure that threatens the distributions the fund is bought for.
Unhedged currency exposure presented as a property play — FX swings that swamp the underlying real-estate return for a single-currency investor.
