greenFlags: Low realised correlation to both stocks and bonds that persists in stress — the diversification mandate met, not just a flattering full-period average.
Transparent exposure to recognised alternative risk premia (trend, carry, value, merger-arb) rather than an opaque black box — you can see and stress-test the return drivers.
A single, reasonable all-in fee with no hidden underlying-fund layer — many multialternative wrappers stack fees that quietly eat the diversification benefit.
Smooth risk-adjusted return across multiple regimes including 2022 — shows the blend isn't secretly long equity/credit beta.
redFlags: Correlation to a 60/40 portfolio that spikes in drawdowns — closet beta that fails to diversify exactly when protection is needed most.
A fund-of-funds fee stack on top of the wrapper — double-layer fees that turn a mediocre raw return into a poor net one.
An ever-changing strategy mix chasing whatever worked recently — performance-chasing that destroys the stable, diversifying profile investors bought.
A long flat-to-negative track record versus simply holding cash plus a cheap trend fund — the complexity and fees add cost without adding the promised non-correlated return.

mostImportant: How it is built — a fund whose explicit job is to cushion equity drawdowns, typically by holding equities alongside a standing put/collar overlay, tail-risk options, or a defensive long-vol/managed-overlay sleeve.

The character of the resulting portfolio — it deliberately trades away some upside (option premium drag, hedge cost) for protection, so it should lag a rising market and lose materially less in selloffs, making it a convexity tool rather than a growth holding.
The nature of its income and tax — option costs and resets create uneven, often 1256-flavoured tax flows, and any yield is incidental; the product is bought for its drawdown profile, not income.
greenFlags: A clearly asymmetric capture profile — small upside give-up for a large reduction in downside (high downside-protection-per-unit-of-upside-forgone) versus the S&P 500.
Hedges that actually pay off and are monetised in a real selloff (2022, Q1 2020) — confirms the protection is live, not a perpetual premium drain that never delivers.
A transparent, rules-based hedge reset (laddered puts, scheduled collar rolls) — you know exactly when and how protection renews rather than relying on manager timing.
Cost-of-carry kept low through spread structures or partial financing — the chronic bleed that kills most tail-hedge funds is being actively managed down.
redFlags: Heavy upside lag in normal markets with little corresponding downside relief — the hedge is all cost and no payoff, the most common way these funds disappoint.
Discretionary hedge timing that was off in the last drawdown — the protection wasn't on when it mattered, defeating the entire purpose.
A static always-on long-vol position bleeding premium every calm year — a structural drag that needs a rare crash just to break even.
Basis risk between the hedge and the held equities (index puts against a different portfolio) — the cushion underperforms exactly in the selloff it was meant to offset.

mostImportant: How it generates returns — trading volatility itself via VIX futures, variance swaps or options, either long-vol (buying protection/convexity) or short-vol (harvesting the volatility-risk premium by selling it), a distinction that defines the entire risk profile.

The character of the resulting portfolio — long-vol funds bleed steadily and spike up in crashes, while short-vol funds earn a steady premium and can lose catastrophically in a vol spike; VIX-futures vehicles also carry persistent roll cost (contango) or gain (backwardation).

The nature of its income and tax — returns come from derivatives with 1256 treatment and are highly path-dependent; these are tactical convexity or premium-harvesting tools, not income or buy-and-hold holdings.
greenFlags: For long-vol: a large, fast positive payoff in vol spikes (Feb 2018, Q1 2020) that outweighs the calm-period bleed — the convexity is doing its job.
For short-vol: position sizing and stop-rules that survived a major spike with a recoverable drawdown — proof the premium harvest isn't a hidden blow-up waiting to happen.
Active roll management or dynamic VIX-curve positioning that limits contango drag — addresses the structural cost that erodes naive volatility products.
A clearly disclosed, stable long-vs-short-vol stance — you know which side of the convexity trade you own rather than holding an ambiguous mix.
redFlags: A short-vol payoff profile that looks like free money for years — picking up pennies in front of a steamroller, with one spike (the 2018 'Volmageddon' VXX/XIV episode) capable of wiping out the fund.
A long-vol product whose contango bleed exceeds its crash payoffs over a full cycle — a perpetual decay machine that loses even across the events it's built for (the classic VXX long-hold trap).
Embedded leverage or inverse exposure (2x/-1x VIX) held longer than intended — compounding and roll decay that guarantee large losses over time regardless of direction.
Opaque switching between long and short vol — the manager can flip the fund's entire risk character without warning, leaving you short convexity right before a spike.
